title: GraphQL Schema-First API
description: GraphQL API designed schema-first: the SDL schema is the authoritative contract authored before implementation, enabling parallel frontend/backend development and strong type safety. Clients request exactly the fields they need, eliminating over-fetching; requires DataLoader-style batching to prevent N+1 resolver queries and a caching strategy for expensive field resolvers.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: GraphQL Schema-First API requires infrastructure for hosting, caching, and monitoring. Min ($50): Small-scale deployment with container hosting (AWS ECS Fargate ~$15-30/month for 0.25 vCPU), basic Redis cache (ElastiCache t3.micro ~$13/month), API Gateway/ALB (~$10-15/month), and minimal data transfer. Suitable for development or low-traffic APIs (<1000 req/day). Max ($3000): Production deployment with auto-scaling containers (ECS/EKS ~$500-800), multi-AZ Redis cluster (~$400-600), GraphQL-specific features like Apollo Router/Federation (~$200-500 managed), CDN caching (~$100-300), comprehensive monitoring/APM (~$200-400), and significant data transfer for high-traffic APIs (>1M req/day). Costs scale with resolver complexity, nested query depth, and N+1 query patterns requiring DataLoader implementations.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity pattern. Schema-first approach requires: (1) Learning GraphQL schema design patterns and SDL (Schema Definition Language) - 20-40 hours for team training, (2) Designing comprehensive type system and schema contracts - 40-60 hours, (3) Implementing efficient resolvers with DataLoader for N+1 prevention - 60-80 hours, (4) Setting up query complexity analysis and depth limiting to prevent abuse - 20-30 hours, (5) Implementing caching strategy (Redis, response caching, persisted queries) - 30-40 hours, (6) Integration testing for schema validation and breaking change detection - 20-30 hours, (7) Performance testing and resolver optimization - 20-30 hours. Total 210-310 hours. At blended rate of $75-100/hour (considering AI coding assistants reduce time by 30-40%), adoption cost ranges $2000-3000. Schema-first requires more upfront design than code-first but provides better API governance.
provenance.licenseCost: GraphQL core tooling is open source and MIT-licensed (GraphQL.js, Apollo Server, GraphQL Yoga, Mercurius). Schema-first tools like GraphQL Code Generator, graphql-tools, and schema stitching libraries are also open source. However, production deployments may optionally use commercial tools: Apollo Studio (free tier available, Team plan $200-500/month for advanced features like schema registry and federated gateway), AWS AppSync (pay-per-use, not license fee), or GraphQL managed services. Base implementation uses entirely free/open-source stack, so license cost is $0. Commercial tooling is optional enhancement, not required for schema-first approach.

provides.reasoning: This pattern implements a GraphQL API with schema-first approach, providing the core GraphQL API capability as the primary interface pattern.
GraphQL's query language allows clients to request exactly the data they need, avoiding over-fetching and under-fetching, which is a core feature distinguishing it from REST APIs.
Schema-first approach means the GraphQL schema serves as a formal contract between frontend and backend, enabling parallel development and clear API boundaries.
Schema-first GraphQL provides strong typing through the schema definition, ensuring type safety across the API contract and enabling code generation.
GraphQL schema-first implementations commonly include DataLoader or equivalent batching mechanisms to address the N+1 query problem, providing query-batching capability to dependent patterns.

requires.reasoning: Schema-first approach requires governance processes to manage schema evolution, versioning, and breaking changes to maintain the schema contract reliability across teams.
The pattern description explicitly states caching is needed since GraphQL queries can be expensive, especially with complex nested queries and multiple resolvers.
The pattern requires resolver performance controls and monitoring to identify bottlenecks, track query complexity, and manage performance issues inherent in GraphQL implementations.

GraphQL implementations commonly face N+1 query problems where nested resolvers trigger multiple database queries. Batching capabilities like DataLoader are essential to prevent performance degradation.
Schema-first GraphQL requires validation capabilities to test schema definitions, ensure query validity, and verify resolver implementations against the schema contract.

supports_nfr.reason: GraphQL resolver chains, query parsing overhead, and DataLoader batching delays contribute minimum 100ms p95 latency floor.
GraphQL resolver CPU overhead and DataLoader batching limit throughput ceiling to 360M jobs/hour (100K/sec) compared to simpler REST APIs.
GraphQL read queries are resolver-bounded but can scale to very high QPS with proper caching and DataLoader optimization strategies.
GraphQL over HTTP supports all standard authentication mechanisms via HTTP headers and resolver context injection.
GraphQL field-level audit logging can be implemented through resolver middleware but is optional based on compliance requirements.
GraphQL resolver context supports all tenant isolation strategies through context-based authorization and data filtering in resolvers.
GraphQL schema-first design handles PII with field-level access control, schema directives, and resolver-level masking or encryption.
GraphQL supports GDPR compliance through schema-defined mutations for data deletion, field-level access control, and audit logging in resolvers.
GraphQL can comply with HIPAA when combined with field-level encryption, resolver-level authorization, and comprehensive audit logging middleware.
GraphQL schema-first design supports CCPA compliance through explicitly typed mutations for data access requests and opt-out operations.
GraphQL resolvers can implement SOX-compliant field-level audit trails and access controls for financial data through middleware patterns.
GraphQL schema-first with synchronous resolver execution supports read-your-writes consistency when resolvers query consistent data sources.

supports_constraints.reason: GraphQL is cloud-agnostic and can run on any cloud provider or on-premises infrastructure as a standard HTTP-based API layer.
GraphQL schema-first API is a backend API pattern that can be implemented in any language with GraphQL server libraries available.
GraphQL APIs are backend services that can be consumed by any platform type including web, mobile, desktop, CLI, and IoT clients.
GraphQL queries benefit significantly from caching strategies (query result caching, DataLoader, persisted queries) making caching effectively required.
GraphQL can work with or without async messaging; subscriptions may use it but the pattern itself is messaging-agnostic.
GraphQL is database-agnostic and works with any data source including document stores through resolver abstraction layer.
GraphQL is database-agnostic and works with any data source including key-value stores for caching or primary storage.
GraphQL APIs commonly serve OLTP workloads through queries and mutations but are not limited to transactional use cases.
GraphQL can serve analytical queries but requires careful performance optimization, query complexity limits, and timeout controls for OLAP workloads.
GraphQL schema-first design supports multi-tenancy through resolver-level authorization, context-based tenant filtering, and schema directives.
GraphQL's graph-like query model maps naturally to graph database traversals through nested resolvers but works with any data source.
GraphQL resolvers can query time-series databases; subscriptions are useful for real-time time-series updates but not required.
GraphQL resolvers can expose vector search operations through typed query fields with embedding input arguments and similarity scoring.
GraphQL mutations can trigger and monitor batch jobs; DataLoader inherently performs batch processing for resolver data fetching.
GraphQL is neutral on distributed transactions; saga or 2PC coordination happens in resolvers or underlying services, not in the API layer.
GraphQL subscriptions over WebSocket natively support real-time streaming use cases but are optional; queries/mutations work without streaming.
GraphQL resolvers can expose AI inference operations; flexible query structure is ideal for model input/output but not AI-specific.

schema_validation.description: Schema validation mode. Strict enforces all type constraints and nullability rules at runtime. Permissive allows more flexibility but may lead to runtime errors.

introspection_enabled.description: Enable GraphQL introspection queries. Useful for development and GraphQL clients, but should be disabled in production for security.

query_depth_limit.description: Maximum nesting depth for queries. Prevents deeply nested queries that could cause performance issues or DoS attacks.

query_complexity_limit.description: Maximum query complexity score based on field weights. Prevents expensive queries from overwhelming resolvers and databases.

caching_strategy.description: Caching granularity. Field-level caches individual resolver results (most flexible), query-level caches entire query results, response-level caches HTTP responses, none disables caching.

batching_enabled.description: Enable DataLoader-style batching to combine multiple resolver requests into single database queries. Reduces N+1 query problems but adds complexity.

persisted_queries.description: Use persisted/automatic persisted queries where clients send query hashes instead of full query strings. Improves performance and security but requires client support.

subscription_transport.description: Transport mechanism for GraphQL subscriptions. WebSocket provides bidirectional real-time communication, SSE is simpler but server-to-client only, polling is most compatible but least efficient.

warn_nfr.reason: GraphQL adds resolver overhead, schema parsing, and DataLoader batching complexity. At low QPS, simpler REST APIs are more efficient.
warn_nfr.message: api-graphql-schema-first: peak read QPS is {actual} req/s (<10). GraphQL overhead (resolver chains, DataLoader, query parsing) may outweigh flexibility benefits. Consider simpler REST API.
